1:
"For this Melchisedec, king of Salem, priest of the most high God, who met Abraham returning from the slaughter of the kings, and blessed him;" The witnesses note Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest, who met and blessed Abraham. On "Melchisedec, king of Salem, priest of the most high God," the grammatical voices note his double office. Barnes and Robertson note the historical figure (Genesis 14:18-20): "Melchisedec, king of Salem (probably Jerusalem, or a city of that name), priest of the most high God" — a king who was also a priest, uniting both offices, and a priest of the true God outside the line of Israel.

On "who met Abraham returning from the slaughter of the kings, and blessed him," the witnesses note the encounter. Barnes and Calvin note the event: Melchizedek "met Abraham returning from the slaughter of the kings (the defeat of the four kings, Genesis 14), and blessed him" — he came out to meet the victorious patriarch and pronounced a blessing on him.

The eastern Chrysostom notes the type: this brief, mysterious appearance of a king-priest of the true God, blessing Abraham, was designed by the Spirit as a type of Christ; the writer takes up the figure to prove the superiority of Christ's priesthood. The verse displays Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest, who met and blessed Abraham.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: his double office — "Melchisedec, king of Salem (probably Jerusalem), priest of the most high God," a king-priest of the true God outside Israel's line (Genesis 14, Barnes, Robertson); "met Abraham returning from the slaughter of the kings, and blessed him," meeting the victorious patriarch and blessing him (Barnes, Calvin); the appearance designed as a type of Christ (Chrysostom). The verse displays Melchizedek. The verse marks the king: of Salem. The verse displays the priest: of the most high God. The verse displays the encounter: met Abraham. The verse displays the blessing: pronounced. The figure displays the king-priest blessing Abraham. The verse displays the Melchizedek: king and priest. The verse displays the meeting: with Abraham. The verse displays the blessing: given. Melchizedek, king of Salem and priest, who met and blessed Abraham, displays the figure the writer takes up: a king who was also priest of the most high God, who met the returning Abraham and blessed him — a mysterious type of Christ's royal priesthood, whose meaning is now unfolded. The verse displays the king-priest, whose name and title are interpreted (v.2).
2:
"To whom also Abraham gave a tenth part of all; first being by interpretation King of righteousness, and after that also King of Salem, which is, King of peace;" The witnesses note the tithe Abraham paid and the meaning of Melchizedek's names. On "to whom also Abraham gave a tenth part of all," the grammatical voices note the tithe. Barnes and Robertson note the significant act: "Abraham gave a tenth part of all (the spoils of his victory)" — the patriarch paid tithes to Melchizedek, an act of homage acknowledging Melchizedek's superior dignity (the point developed in vv.4-10).

On "first being by interpretation King of righteousness," the witnesses note the name. Barnes and Calvin note the meaning of "Melchizedek" (melek-tsedek): "King of righteousness" — his very name signifies righteousness; a fitting type of Christ, the true King of righteousness, who is and brings righteousness.

On "King of Salem, which is, King of peace," the grammatical-historical note the title: "King of Salem (shalem)" means "King of peace" — his title signifies peace; so the order is righteousness first, then peace — as in Christ, who first establishes righteousness and then peace (cf. Romans 5:1; Isaiah 32:17). The verse displays the tithe Abraham paid and the meaning of Melchizedek's names.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the tithe — "Abraham gave a tenth part of all (the spoils)," paying tithes to Melchizedek as homage to his superior dignity (Barnes, Robertson); "King of righteousness," the meaning of Melchizedek's name, a type of Christ the King of righteousness (Barnes, Calvin); "King of Salem, King of peace," his title signifying peace, the order righteousness then peace, as in Christ (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the names and tithe. The verse marks the tithe: a tenth of all. The verse displays the name: King of righteousness. The verse displays the title: King of peace. The verse displays the order: righteousness then peace. The meaning displays Melchizedek's names typifying Christ. The verse displays the tenth: paid. The verse displays the righteousness: his name. The verse displays the peace: his title. The tithe Abraham paid and the meaning of Melchizedek's names display the typical significance of the figure: Abraham paid him a tenth in homage, and his name and title — King of righteousness and King of peace — foreshadow Christ, who first brings righteousness and then peace. The verse displays the meaning of the names, deepened by the silence of the record (v.3).
3:
"Without father, without mother, without descent, having neither beginning of days, nor end of life; but made like unto the Son of God; abideth a priest continually." The witnesses note Melchizedek without recorded genealogy, made like the Son of God — the crux of his nature. On the meaning of "without father, without mother, without descent," interpreters divide over Melchizedek's nature. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott, with the Reformed Calvin and Gill, hold that these phrases describe not his actual being but the silence of the Scripture record: Melchizedek was a real man who had parents and was born and died, but Scripture (which carefully records the genealogies of the Levitical priests) mentions no father, mother, genealogy, birth, or death for him — so that, in the record, he stands alone, "without genealogy," appearing suddenly as a perpetual priest; the argument is from the silence of the text. A rejected view (noted by Ellicott and Calvin — the third-century Melchizedekians) held him a superhuman being — the Son of God himself, the Holy Spirit, or an angel; Calvin calls these "delirious notions." A further view (Jewish, noted and rejected by Calvin) identified him with Shem the son of Noah.

On "made like unto the Son of God; abideth a priest continually," the witnesses note the type. Barnes and Calvin note that Melchizedek was "made like (aphomoiomenos — formed as a copy, a type) unto the Son of God" and "abideth a priest continually" — as far as the record goes, with no recorded death or successor, he is a standing type of Christ's perpetual priesthood; the phrase "made like unto the Son of God" itself proves he was not the Son (one is not "made like" oneself). The verse displays Melchizedek without recorded genealogy, made like the Son of God.

The witnesses agree the substance: Melchizedek, by the silence of the record (no father, mother, genealogy, birth, or death mentioned), stands in Scripture as a solitary, perpetual priest — a divinely-designed type of the Son of God, whose priesthood is without beginning or end (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Gill, Ellicott). But his nature has divided interpreters: the consensus reads the phrases of the record's silence (a real man, typically presented); the Melchizedekians held him a theophany of the Son, Spirit, or an angel (rejected by Calvin as "delirious"); Jewish tradition identified him with Shem (rejected). The verse displays the Melchizedek crux. The verse marks the silence: no genealogy. The verse displays the debate: a real man typified, or a theophany, or Shem. The verse displays the type: made like the Son of God. The verse displays the priesthood: continually. The crux displays Melchizedek as a type by the record's silence. The verse displays the father: none recorded. The verse displays the likeness: to the Son. The verse displays the abiding: a priest. Melchizedek without recorded genealogy, made like the Son of God, displays the heart of the type and a famous crux: the silence of Scripture concerning his father, mother, genealogy, birth, and death makes him, in the record, a perpetual priest like the Son of God — read by the consensus as the record's deliberate silence about a real man (Barnes, Calvin, Gill), and rejected when taken (by the Melchizedekians) as a theophany of the Son, Spirit, or angel, or (by Jewish tradition) as Shem. The verse displays the type of the eternal priest, whose greatness is now weighed (v.4).
4:
"Now consider how great this man was, unto whom even the patriarch Abraham gave the tenth of the spoils." The brief verse notes the greatness of Melchizedek, to whom Abraham paid tithes. The grammatical voices note the argument from the tithe. Barnes and Robertson note the writer's appeal: "consider (behold, ponder) how great this man was, unto whom even the patriarch Abraham gave the tenth of the spoils" — the greatness of Melchizedek is proved by the fact that Abraham, the venerated patriarch, paid him tithes.

On "even the patriarch Abraham," the witnesses note the eminence of the giver. Barnes and Henry note the force of "even the patriarch Abraham" — not an ordinary man, but the great father of the nation, the friend of God, the one to whom the promises were given; that such a man paid tithes to Melchizedek shows Melchizedek's surpassing dignity.

On "gave the tenth of the spoils," the grammatical-historical note the act of homage: to pay a tenth was to acknowledge a superior; Abraham's tithe was homage to Melchizedek's greater dignity — the foundation of the argument that Melchizedek (and so Christ's priesthood) is greater than the Levitical. The verse displays the greatness of Melchizedek.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appeal — "consider how great this man was, unto whom even the patriarch Abraham gave the tenth of the spoils," Melchizedek's greatness proved by Abraham's tithe (Barnes, Robertson); "even the patriarch Abraham," the great father of the nation, whose tithe shows Melchizedek's surpassing dignity (Barnes, Henry); the tithe an act of homage acknowledging a superior (grammatical-historical). The verse displays his greatness. The verse marks the consideration: how great. The verse displays the giver: even Abraham. The verse displays the tithe: of the spoils. The verse displays the homage: to a superior. The greatness displays Melchizedek honored by Abraham himself. The verse displays the greatness: weighed. The verse displays the patriarch: tithing. The verse displays the spoils: a tenth. The greatness of Melchizedek, to whom Abraham paid tithes, displays the foundation of the argument: how great he must have been, that even the patriarch Abraham — the father of the faithful — paid him a tenth of the spoils in homage, acknowledging his superior dignity. The verse displays Melchizedek's eminence, greater than the tithe-receiving Levites (vv.5-6).
5:
"And verily they that are of the sons of Levi, who receive the office of the priesthood, have a commandment to take tithes of the people according to the law, that is, of their brethren, though they come out of the loins of Abraham:" The brief verse notes the Levites taking tithes of their brethren by law. The grammatical voices note the Levitical right. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast being set up: "they that are of the sons of Levi, who receive the office of the priesthood, have a commandment to take tithes of the people according to the law" — the Levitical priests had a legal right to receive tithes from the people of Israel.

On "that is, of their brethren," the witnesses note the relation. Barnes and Calvin note that the Levites took tithes "of their brethren (fellow-Israelites)" — those they tithed were their own kinsmen, descendants of the same father; the tithing was within the family of Israel, brother taking from brother.

On "though they come out of the loins of Abraham," the grammatical-historical note the common descent: both the tithing Levites and the tithed people "come out of the loins of Abraham" — all alike descended from Abraham; the Levites' right to tithe rested on the law, not on any superiority of nature, for they and their brethren shared one ancestor. The verse displays the Levites taking tithes of their brethren by law.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Levitical right — "the sons of Levi who receive the priesthood have a commandment to take tithes of the people according to the law," the priests' legal right to tithes from Israel (Barnes, Robertson); "of their brethren," the tithed being fellow-Israelites, kinsmen, brother from brother (Barnes, Calvin); "though they come out of the loins of Abraham," both tithers and tithed descended from Abraham, the right resting on law not superior nature (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Levitical tithe. The verse marks the priests: sons of Levi. The verse displays the right: take tithes by law. The verse displays the tithed: their brethren. The verse displays the descent: of Abraham. The tithe displays the Levites taking from their kinsmen by law. The verse displays the Levites: tithing. The verse displays the brethren: tithed. The verse displays the Abraham: common father. The Levites taking tithes of their brethren by law displays the comparison the writer draws: the Levitical priests, by legal commandment, took tithes from their own brethren — fellow-descendants of Abraham — their right resting on the law, within the one family of Israel. The verse displays the Levitical tithe, surpassed by Melchizedek's tithe of Abraham himself (v.6).
6:
"But he whose descent is not counted from them received tithes of Abraham, and blessed him that had the promises." The brief verse notes Melchizedek tithing and blessing Abraham, who had the promises. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the superiority shown: "he whose descent is not counted from them (Melchizedek, not of Levi's line, outside the family) received tithes of Abraham (not of mere brethren, but of the patriarch himself)" — Melchizedek, a stranger to the Levitical line, took tithes from Abraham himself, not merely from Abraham's descendants.

On "received tithes of Abraham," the witnesses note the greater act. Barnes and Calvin note the force: the Levites tithed their brethren, but Melchizedek tithed Abraham — the father of the Levites; to receive tithes from Abraham was a greater dignity than to receive them from his descendants.

On "blessed him that had the promises," the grammatical-historical note the second act of superiority: Melchizedek "blessed him that had the promises (Abraham, the recipient of God's great promises)" — and the one who blesses is greater than the one blessed (v.7); that Melchizedek blessed even Abraham, the heir of the promises, shows his surpassing dignity. The verse displays Melchizedek tithing and blessing Abraham.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "he whose descent is not counted from them (Melchizedek, outside Levi's line) received tithes of Abraham," taking tithes from the patriarch himself, not mere brethren (Barnes, Robertson); the Levites tithing their brethren but Melchizedek tithing Abraham, the father of the Levites, a greater dignity (Barnes, Calvin); "blessed him that had the promises," blessing Abraham the heir of the promises, the blesser greater than the blessed (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Melchizedek's superiority. The verse marks the descent: not of Levi. The verse displays the tithe: of Abraham himself. The verse displays the blessing: of Abraham. The verse displays the dignity: surpassing. The acts display Melchizedek greater than Abraham. The verse displays the tithing: of the patriarch. The verse displays the blessing: of the heir. The verse displays the promises: Abraham's. Melchizedek tithing and blessing Abraham, who had the promises, displays his surpassing dignity: a stranger to Levi's line, he received tithes from Abraham himself — not from mere descendants — and blessed the very man who held God's promises, the blesser being greater than the blessed. The verse displays Melchizedek above Abraham, by the rule that the less is blessed of the greater (v.7).
7:
"And without all contradiction the less is blessed of the better." The brief verse notes the principle that the less is blessed by the greater. The grammatical voices note the axiom. Barnes and Robertson note the undisputed rule the argument rests on: "without all contradiction (beyond dispute, indisputably) the less (the inferior) is blessed of (by) the better (the superior)" — it is a recognized principle that the one who gives a blessing is greater than the one who receives it.

On "the less is blessed of the better," the witnesses note the application. Barnes and Calvin note the bearing: since Melchizedek blessed Abraham (v.6), and the blesser is greater than the blessed, Melchizedek is greater than Abraham — and so greater than Levi and the Levitical priesthood descended from Abraham; the axiom clinches the argument for Melchizedek's superiority.

The eastern Chrysostom notes the force: the act of blessing, in the patriarchal sense, was an exercise of superior authority (as a father blesses his children, or a priest the people); that Melchizedek blessed Abraham places him above the patriarch beyond contradiction. The verse displays the principle that the less is blessed by the greater.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the axiom — "without all contradiction (indisputably) the less (inferior) is blessed of (by) the better (superior)," the blesser greater than the blessed (Barnes, Robertson); the application, Melchizedek blessing Abraham proving Melchizedek greater than Abraham and the Levites (Barnes, Calvin); blessing an exercise of superior authority, placing Melchizedek above the patriarch (Chrysostom). The verse displays the axiom. The verse marks the rule: the less blessed of the better. The verse displays the certainty: without contradiction. The verse displays the application: Melchizedek above Abraham. The verse displays the authority: of the blesser. The principle displays the blesser's superiority over the blessed. The verse displays the less: blessed. The verse displays the better: blessing. The verse displays the rule: indisputable. The principle that the less is blessed by the greater displays the axiom that clinches the argument: beyond all dispute the one who blesses is superior to the one blessed — so that Melchizedek, having blessed Abraham, is shown greater than the patriarch and the priesthood descended from him. The verse displays the rule of blessing, applied to the dying Levites and the living Melchizedek (v.8).
8:
"And here men that die receive tithes; but there he receiveth them, of whom it is witnessed that he liveth." The brief verse notes the dying Levites and the living Melchizedek receiving tithes. The grammatical voices note the contrast of mortality. Barnes and Robertson note the further point: "here (in the Levitical system) men that die receive tithes" — the Levitical priests who take tithes are mortal men, who die and pass on their office; their priesthood is marked by death and succession.

On "but there he receiveth them, of whom it is witnessed that he liveth," the witnesses note the living priest. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast: "there (in the case of Melchizedek) he receiveth them, of whom it is witnessed (by the silence of the record) that he liveth" — Melchizedek, of whose death Scripture says nothing, is presented as one who lives; the record bears witness, by its silence, to a continuing, undying priesthood.

On "of whom it is witnessed that he liveth," the grammatical-historical note the type: this living, undying quality (in the record) makes Melchizedek a type of Christ, the living priest who abides for ever; the contrast between the dying Levites and the "living" Melchizedek foreshadows the contrast between the mortal Levitical priesthood and Christ's eternal one. The verse displays the dying Levites and the living Melchizedek.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "here men that die receive tithes," the Levitical priests mortal, marked by death and succession (Barnes, Robertson); "there he receiveth them, of whom it is witnessed (by the record's silence) that he liveth," Melchizedek presented as living, no death recorded (Barnes, Calvin); this living quality typifying Christ the living, eternal priest (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the contrast. The verse marks the Levites: men that die. The verse displays the Melchizedek: living. The verse displays the witness: the record's silence. The verse displays the type: the eternal priest. The contrast displays the mortal priests and the undying type. The verse displays the dying: the Levites. The verse displays the living: Melchizedek. The verse displays the tithes: received. The dying Levites and the living Melchizedek receiving tithes display a further mark of superiority: the Levitical tithe-receivers are mortal men who die, but Melchizedek, of whose death Scripture is silent, is presented as living — a type of Christ, the undying priest who receives tithes and abides for ever. The verse displays the living priest, in whom even Levi paid tithes (vv.9-10).
9:
"And as I may so say, Levi also, who receiveth tithes, payed tithes in Abraham." The brief verse notes that Levi himself paid tithes in Abraham. The grammatical voices note the careful claim. Barnes and Robertson note the writer's guarded statement: "as I may so say (so to speak, with a qualifying phrase that softens a bold assertion), Levi also, who receiveth tithes, payed tithes in Abraham" — in a sense, even Levi (the ancestor of the tithe-receiving priests) paid tithes to Melchizedek, through his forefather Abraham.

On "as I may so say," the witnesses note the qualification. Barnes and Calvin note that the writer adds "as I may so say" to mark that this is a manner of speaking, not a literal act of Levi; he guards the bold figure, acknowledging it is said in a representative or seminal sense.

On "Levi... payed tithes in Abraham," the grammatical-historical note the seminal idea: Levi, being yet unborn but contained in Abraham (v.10), is reckoned to have paid tithes when Abraham did; the descendant acted, representatively, in the ancestor — so that the tithe-receiving Levites themselves, in their father, acknowledged Melchizedek's superiority. The verse displays that Levi himself paid tithes in Abraham.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the guarded statement — "as I may so say (so to speak), Levi also, who receiveth tithes, payed tithes in Abraham," even Levi paying tithes to Melchizedek through Abraham (Barnes, Robertson); "as I may so say" marking a manner of speaking, not a literal act, guarding the bold figure (Barnes, Calvin); Levi, contained in Abraham, reckoned to have paid representatively (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Levi's tithe. The verse marks the qualification: as I may so say. The verse displays the payer: Levi. The verse displays the means: in Abraham. The verse displays the sense: representative. The claim displays Levi tithing through his ancestor. The verse displays the Levi: paying. The verse displays the tithes: in Abraham. The verse displays the figure: guarded. That Levi himself paid tithes in Abraham displays the writer's striking conclusion: in a manner of speaking, even Levi — the ancestor of the tithe-receiving priests — paid tithes to Melchizedek, since he was yet in Abraham when Abraham paid; the priesthood itself, in its father, owned Melchizedek's superiority. The verse displays Levi's seminal tithe, grounded in his being in Abraham's loins (v.10).
10:
"For he was yet in the loins of his father, when Melchisedec met him." The brief verse notes Levi yet in Abraham's loins when Melchizedek met him. The grammatical voices note the explanation. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of the previous verse: "he (Levi) was yet in the loins of his father (Abraham), when Melchisedec met him" — Levi was not yet born, but existed seminally in Abraham, his great-grandfather; he was, as it were, present in Abraham when the tithe was paid.

On "in the loins of his father," the witnesses note the seminal idea. Barnes and Calvin note the principle of seminal or representative existence: the descendant is regarded as contained in the ancestor; so Levi, in Abraham's loins, is reckoned to have shared in Abraham's act of paying tithes; the head acts for the posterity yet unborn.

On "when Melchisedec met him," the grammatical-historical note the conclusion: at the moment Melchizedek met Abraham, Levi was potentially present; thus the whole Levitical priesthood, contained in Abraham, paid homage to Melchizedek — proving that Melchizedek's priesthood (and Christ's, after his order) is superior to the Levitical. The verse displays Levi yet in Abraham's loins when Melchizedek met him.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the explanation — "he (Levi) was yet in the loins of his father (Abraham) when Melchisedec met him," Levi unborn but seminally present in Abraham (Barnes, Robertson); the principle of seminal existence, the descendant contained in the ancestor, sharing his act (Barnes, Calvin); the whole Levitical priesthood, in Abraham, paying homage to Melchizedek, proving his superiority (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Levi in Abraham. The verse marks the existence: in the loins. The verse displays the father: Abraham. The verse displays the moment: Melchizedek met him. The verse displays the conclusion: the priesthood's homage. The explanation displays Levi present in Abraham at the tithe. The verse displays the loins: Abraham's. The verse displays the Levi: unborn. The verse displays the homage: paid. That Levi was yet in Abraham's loins when Melchizedek met him displays the ground of the argument: Levi, though unborn, existed seminally in Abraham — so that when Abraham paid tithes, the whole Levitical priesthood, contained in him, paid homage to Melchizedek, proving the superiority of his order. The verse displays the seminal homage, closing the argument for a better priesthood (v.11).
11:
"If therefore perfection were by the Levitical priesthood, (for under it the people received the law,) what further need was there that another priest should rise after the order of Melchisedec, and not be called after the order of Aaron?" The witnesses note the imperfection of the Levitical priesthood proved by the need of another. On "if perfection were by the Levitical priesthood," the grammatical voices note the argument. Barnes and Robertson note the reasoning: "if perfection (teleiosis — completeness, the full bringing of men to God, complete reconciliation and access) were by the Levitical priesthood" — if the Levitical system could have made men perfect, accomplishing full salvation, there would be no need of another.

On "for under it the people received the law," the witnesses note the parenthesis. Barnes and Calvin note that the whole Mosaic law was bound up with the Levitical priesthood ("under it the people received the law") — the priesthood and the law stood or fell together; the law was given on the basis of that priesthood.

On "what further need... that another priest should rise after the order of Melchisedec," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the conclusion: the fact that God promised "another priest after the order of Melchisedec, and not after the order of Aaron" (Psalm 110:4) proves that the Levitical priesthood was imperfect and insufficient; had it sufficed, no new and different priesthood would have been needed. The verse displays the imperfection of the Levitical priesthood.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the argument — "if perfection (complete salvation, full access to God) were by the Levitical priesthood," there would be no need of another (Barnes, Robertson); "under it the people received the law," priesthood and law bound together, standing or falling as one (Barnes, Calvin); the promise of "another priest after the order of Melchisedec, not of Aaron" (Psalm 110:4) proving the Levitical priesthood imperfect (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the imperfection. The verse marks the test: perfection by the Levitical. The verse displays the law: under the priesthood. The verse displays the proof: another priest needed. The verse displays the order: Melchisedec, not Aaron. The argument displays the old priesthood shown insufficient. The verse displays the perfection: not attained. The verse displays the priesthood: imperfect. The verse displays the new priest: needed. The imperfection of the Levitical priesthood proved by the need of another displays the heart of the chapter's argument: had the Levitical priesthood — with which the whole law stood — been able to bring perfection, no other would be needed; but God promised another priest after the order of Melchizedek, not Aaron — proving the old priesthood imperfect. The verse displays the insufficient priesthood, whose change requires a change of law (v.12).
12:
"For the priesthood being changed, there is made of necessity a change also of the law." The brief verse notes that a change of priesthood requires a change of law. The grammatical voices note the inseparable link. Barnes and Robertson note the inference: "the priesthood being changed (transferred, from Aaron's line to Melchizedek's order), there is made of necessity a change (metathesis — alteration, transfer) also of the law" — since the law and the priesthood were bound together (v.11), to change the priesthood is necessarily to change the law.

On "the priesthood being changed," the witnesses note the transfer. Barnes and Calvin note that the priesthood has been changed — taken from the Levitical order and given to Christ after the order of Melchizedek; this is no small thing, for the whole legal system rested on the priesthood.

On "a change also of the law," the grammatical-historical note the consequence: because the ceremonial law was inseparably tied to the Levitical priesthood, the change of priesthood involves the abrogation or transformation of that law; the old covenant order passes with the priesthood on which it rested. The verse displays that a change of priesthood requires a change of law.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inference — "the priesthood being changed (transferred to Melchizedek's order), there is made of necessity a change (alteration) also of the law," priesthood and law bound together so that changing one changes the other (Barnes, Robertson); the priesthood taken from Levi and given to Christ, no small thing (Barnes, Calvin); the ceremonial law tied to the priesthood, abrogated or transformed with it (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the change. The verse marks the priesthood: changed. The verse displays the necessity: a change of law. The verse displays the link: inseparable. The verse displays the consequence: the old order passes. The inference displays the law changing with the priesthood. The verse displays the changing: of the priesthood. The verse displays the law: changed. The verse displays the necessity: certain. That a change of priesthood requires a change of law displays the far-reaching consequence of Christ's priesthood: since the priesthood has been transferred from Levi's line to the order of Melchizedek, and the whole law rested on that priesthood, of necessity the law itself is changed — the old covenant order passing with the priesthood on which it stood. The verse displays the changed law, since Christ sprang from another tribe (vv.13-14).
13:
"For he of whom these things are spoken pertaineth to another tribe, of which no man gave attendance at the altar." The brief verse notes that Christ belonged to another tribe than the priestly one. The grammatical voices note the proof of the change. Barnes and Robertson note the argument from Christ's tribe: "he of whom these things are spoken (Christ, the new priest) pertaineth to (belongs to, is a member of) another tribe, of which no man gave attendance at the altar" — Christ came from a tribe that had no part in the priesthood; no member of his tribe ever served at the altar.

On "another tribe," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ's priesthood, belonging to a non-priestly tribe (Judah, v.14), is necessarily a different priesthood from the Levitical — for under the law only Levites could serve; Christ's priesthood breaks the Levitical pattern.

On "no man gave attendance at the altar," the grammatical-historical note the exclusion: from Christ's tribe "no man gave attendance at the altar" — none of that tribe ever officiated as priest; the priestly service was confined to Levi. That the Messiah-priest came from outside Levi proves a change of priesthood and so of law. The verse displays that Christ belonged to another tribe.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the argument — "he of whom these things are spoken (Christ) pertaineth to another tribe, of which no man gave attendance at the altar," Christ from a non-priestly tribe (Barnes, Robertson); his priesthood, from a non-priestly tribe, necessarily different from the Levitical, breaking the pattern (Barnes, Calvin); "no man gave attendance at the altar," the priestly service confined to Levi, the Messiah-priest from outside it (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the other tribe. The verse marks the priest: of another tribe. The verse displays the exclusion: none served at the altar. The verse displays the proof: a different priesthood. The verse displays the consequence: the law changed. The argument displays Christ's priesthood outside the Levitical line. The verse displays the tribe: another. The verse displays the altar: not served. The verse displays the change: proved. That Christ belonged to another tribe than the priestly one displays the proof of the changed priesthood: the new priest came from a tribe of which no member ever served at the altar — so his priesthood cannot be Levitical but is of another, higher order, requiring the change of law. The verse displays the non-Levitical priest, sprung evidently from Judah (v.14).
14:
"For it is evident that our Lord sprang out of Juda; of which tribe Moses spake nothing concerning priesthood." The brief verse notes that Christ sprang from Judah, a non-priestly tribe. The grammatical voices note the well-known fact. Barnes and Robertson note the plain truth: "it is evident (manifest, beyond dispute) that our Lord sprang out of Juda (the tribe of Judah)" — Christ's descent from Judah, the royal tribe (the line of David), was undisputed and well known.

On "sprang out of Juda," the witnesses note the royal line. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ came from Judah — the kingly, not the priestly, tribe; his royalty was of Judah, his priesthood not of Levi; he is king after Judah and priest after Melchizedek.

On "of which tribe Moses spake nothing concerning priesthood," the grammatical-historical note the silence of the law: "Moses spake nothing concerning priesthood" in connection with Judah — the law assigned the priesthood to Levi alone and said nothing of priests from Judah; so Christ's priesthood, from Judah, is wholly outside the Mosaic provision, of a different and higher order. The verse displays that Christ sprang from Judah.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plain fact — "it is evident that our Lord sprang out of Juda," Christ's descent from Judah undisputed (Barnes, Robertson); Christ from the royal tribe, his royalty of Judah and priesthood not of Levi (Barnes, Calvin); "Moses spake nothing concerning priesthood" of Judah, the law confining priesthood to Levi, Christ's priesthood outside the Mosaic provision (grammatical-historical). The verse displays Christ of Judah. The verse marks the descent: out of Judah. The verse displays the tribe: royal, not priestly. The verse displays the silence: Moses said nothing. The verse displays the order: outside the law. The fact displays Christ's priesthood from a non-priestly tribe. The verse displays the Lord: of Judah. The verse displays the Judah: royal. The verse displays the priesthood: not assigned it. That Christ sprang from Judah, a non-priestly tribe, displays the confirmation of the changed priesthood: it is evident that our Lord came from Judah, the royal tribe, of which Moses said nothing concerning priesthood — so Christ's priesthood, lying wholly outside the Levitical provision, must be of another and higher order. The verse displays the priest from Judah, far more evident in the likeness of Melchizedek (v.15).
15:
"And it is yet far more evident: for that after the similitude of Melchisedec there ariseth another priest," The brief verse notes another priest arising after the likeness of Melchizedek. The grammatical voices note the further proof. Barnes and Robertson note the heightened argument: "it is yet far more evident (more abundantly clear) for that after the similitude (likeness, pattern) of Melchisedec there ariseth (rises up, comes on the scene) another priest" — the change of priesthood is even more plainly shown by the rising of a new priest in the likeness of Melchizedek, not of Aaron.

On "after the similitude of Melchisedec," the witnesses note the pattern. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ arises "after the similitude of Melchizedek" — modeled on Melchizedek's priesthood (royal, personal, perpetual), not the hereditary Levitical pattern; the resemblance to Melchizedek defines the new priesthood.

On "another priest," the grammatical-historical note the newness: "another (heteros — of a different kind) priest" — not merely another in succession but a priest of a wholly different order; the very arising of such a priest confirms that the Levitical order has given way. The verse displays another priest arising after the likeness of Melchizedek.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the heightened argument — "it is yet far more evident, for that after the similitude (likeness) of Melchisedec there ariseth another priest," the change shown by a new priest in Melchizedek's likeness (Barnes, Robertson); Christ modeled on Melchizedek (royal, perpetual), not the Levitical pattern (Barnes, Calvin); "another (of a different kind) priest," of a wholly different order, confirming the Levitical order has given way (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the new priest. The verse marks the evidence: far more evident. The verse displays the pattern: Melchizedek's likeness. The verse displays the priest: another. The verse displays the order: different. The arising displays a new priest of Melchizedek's kind. The verse displays the similitude: of Melchizedek. The verse displays the rising: of a priest. The verse displays the newness: of order. Another priest arising after the likeness of Melchizedek displays the clearer proof of the change: it is yet more evident that the priesthood has changed, for a priest of a wholly different kind — after the pattern of Melchizedek, not Aaron — has arisen. The verse displays the Melchizedek-like priest, constituted by the power of an endless life (v.16).
16:
"Who is made, not after the law of a carnal commandment, but after the power of an endless life." The witnesses note Christ made priest by the power of an endless life. On "not after the law of a carnal commandment," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the negative: Christ "is made (constituted priest) not after the law of a carnal commandment (sarkines — a fleshly, external commandment, the legal rule of physical descent that constituted the Levitical priests)" — his priesthood does not rest on the law of fleshly lineage, as the Levitical did (which required descent from Aaron).

On "a carnal commandment," the witnesses note the Levitical basis. Barnes and Calvin note that the Levitical priesthood was constituted by "a carnal commandment" — an outward, legal rule based on physical descent, mortality, and ceremony; it was earthly and transient in its ground.

On "but after the power of an endless life," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the new ground: Christ is priest "after the power of an endless (akatalytos — indissoluble, indestructible) life" — his priesthood rests not on lineage or law but on the power of an imperishable life; because he lives for ever (having conquered death), his priesthood is perpetual and unfailing. The verse displays Christ made priest by the power of an endless life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "not after the law of a carnal commandment (a fleshly, external rule of descent)," Christ's priesthood not resting on fleshly lineage as the Levitical did (Barnes, Robertson); the Levitical priesthood constituted by an outward rule of descent and ceremony, earthly and transient (Barnes, Calvin); "but after the power of an endless (indissoluble) life," Christ priest by the power of an imperishable life, perpetual because he lives for ever (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the endless-life priesthood. The verse marks the negative: not a carnal commandment. The verse displays the Levitical basis: fleshly descent. The verse displays the new ground: power of an endless life. The verse displays the perpetuity: he ever lives. The contrast displays Christ priest by indestructible life. The verse displays the commandment: not fleshly. The verse displays the life: endless. The verse displays the power: imperishable. Christ made priest by the power of an endless life displays the radical newness of his priesthood: it rests not on the fleshly, legal rule of descent that constituted the mortal Levitical priests, but on the power of an indissoluble life — because he lives for ever, his priesthood is unfailing and perpetual. The verse displays the priest of endless life, witnessed by the eternal oath (v.17).
17:
"For he testifieth, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec." The brief verse notes the testimony of an eternal priesthood after Melchizedek. The grammatical voices note the proof-text. Barnes and Robertson note the Scripture cited (Psalm 110:4): "he (God, in the Psalm) testifieth, Thou art a priest for ever (eis ton aiona — perpetually, eternally) after the order of Melchisedec" — God's own word declares the Messiah a perpetual priest of the Melchizedek order; the foundation of the whole argument.

On "a priest for ever," the witnesses note the perpetuity. Barnes and Calvin note the key word "for ever" — the priesthood is eternal, unending; this is what distinguishes it from the Levitical (which ended at death) and what is proved by Christ's endless life (v.16); the everlasting priesthood is the heart of the matter.

On "after the order of Melchisedec," the eastern Cyril notes the order: the priesthood is "after the order of Melchisedec" — royal, personal, perpetual, not the hereditary Aaronic; Christ fulfills the Psalm as the eternal priest-king after Melchizedek's pattern. The verse displays the testimony of an eternal priesthood after Melchizedek.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the proof-text (Psalm 110:4) — "he testifieth, Thou art a priest for ever (perpetually) after the order of Melchisedec," God's word declaring the Messiah a perpetual priest of Melchizedek's order (Barnes, Robertson); "for ever," the eternal priesthood distinguishing it from the Levitical, proved by his endless life (Barnes, Calvin); "after the order of Melchisedec," royal and perpetual, Christ the eternal priest-king (Cyril). The verse displays the testimony. The verse marks the witness: he testifieth. The verse displays the priesthood: for ever. The verse displays the order: Melchisedec. The verse displays the foundation: Psalm 110:4. The testimony displays God's word of an eternal priesthood. The verse displays the testifying: God's. The verse displays the for ever: eternal. The verse displays the order: Melchizedek's. The testimony of an eternal priesthood after Melchizedek displays the Scripture that grounds the whole argument: God himself declares the Messiah "a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek" — an everlasting priesthood, distinct from the Levitical, fulfilled in Christ who lives for ever. The verse displays the eternal priesthood, which disannuls the weak old commandment (v.18).
18:
"For there is verily a disannulling of the commandment going before for the weakness and unprofitableness thereof." The brief verse notes the setting aside of the weak and unprofitable old commandment. The grammatical voices note the abrogation. Barnes and Robertson note the consequence of the new priesthood: "there is verily a disannulling (athetesis — a setting aside, abrogation, annulment) of the commandment going before (the former commandment, the Levitical law and priesthood)" — the old commandment is set aside, repealed, by the coming of the new priesthood.

On "the commandment going before," the witnesses note the old order. Barnes and Calvin note that "the commandment going before" is the Mosaic law of the priesthood (and the ceremonial system bound to it) — that which preceded the new order; it is now annulled, having served its temporary purpose.

On "for the weakness and unprofitableness thereof," the grammatical-historical note the reason: the old commandment is set aside "for the weakness (it could not give life or strength) and unprofitableness (it could not accomplish salvation, bring men to God) thereof" — not that the law was evil, but that it was weak and could not perfect; it could point to but not provide the reality. The verse displays the setting aside of the weak old commandment.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the abrogation — "there is verily a disannulling (setting aside, repeal) of the commandment going before," the old commandment set aside by the new priesthood (Barnes, Robertson); "the commandment going before" the Mosaic law of priesthood and ceremony, now annulled (Barnes, Calvin); "for the weakness and unprofitableness thereof," set aside because weak and unable to perfect, not evil but insufficient (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the disannulling. The verse marks the setting aside: of the commandment. The verse displays the old: going before. The verse displays the reason: weak and unprofitable. The verse displays the inability: could not perfect. The abrogation displays the old law repealed for its weakness. The verse displays the commandment: disannulled. The verse displays the weakness: its fault. The verse displays the unprofitableness: its lack. The setting aside of the weak and unprofitable old commandment displays the result of the new priesthood: the former commandment — the Mosaic law of the priesthood — is annulled and set aside, because of its weakness and inability to bring men to God; it pointed to, but could not provide, the perfection now come in Christ. The verse displays the abrogated law, replaced by a better hope (v.19).
19:
"For the law made nothing perfect, but the bringing in of a better hope did; by the which we draw nigh unto God." The witnesses note the law making nothing perfect, replaced by a better hope. On "the law made nothing perfect," the grammatical voices note the law's limitation. Barnes and Robertson note the reason for its abrogation: "the law made nothing perfect (brought nothing to completion, accomplished no full reconciliation or access to God)" — the Mosaic law could not perfect the worshipper, could not give a clear conscience or full access; it was preparatory, not final.

On "made nothing perfect," the witnesses note the incompleteness. Barnes and Calvin note that the law, with its sacrifices and priesthood, could not make the comer perfect — could not truly take away sin or bring men fully to God; its work was to reveal sin and point forward, not to complete.

On "the bringing in of a better hope did; by the which we draw nigh unto God," the grammatical-historical note the new and better thing: "the bringing in (introduction, epeisagoge) of a better hope (the gospel hope, grounded in Christ's eternal priesthood) did (perfect what the law could not), by the which we draw nigh (engidzo — approach, have access) unto God" — the better hope, brought in alongside (and superseding) the law, gives what the law could not: real access to God; through it the believer draws near. The verse displays the law making nothing perfect, replaced by a better hope.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the law's limitation — "the law made nothing perfect (accomplished no full reconciliation)," unable to perfect the worshipper or give full access (Barnes, Robertson); the law unable to take away sin or bring men fully to God, preparatory not final (Barnes, Calvin); "the bringing in of a better hope... by the which we draw nigh unto God," the gospel hope giving the real access the law could not (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the better hope. The verse marks the law: made nothing perfect. The verse displays the limitation: no full access. The verse displays the new: a better hope. The verse displays the access: draw nigh to God. The contrast displays the better hope giving what the law could not. The verse displays the law: imperfect. The verse displays the hope: better. The verse displays the drawing nigh: to God. The law making nothing perfect, replaced by a better hope, displays the reason the old order gave way: the law could perfect nothing — it could not bring men fully to God — but the introduction of a better hope, grounded in Christ's eternal priesthood, accomplishes what the law could not, so that through it we draw near to God. The verse displays the better hope, confirmed by God's oath (vv.20-21).
20:
"And inasmuch as not without an oath he was made priest:" The brief verse notes Christ made priest with an oath, unlike the Levites. The grammatical voices note the added confirmation. Barnes and Robertson note the new point of superiority: "inasmuch as not without an oath he was made priest" — Christ's priesthood was established with an oath (Psalm 110:4, "the Lord sware"), unlike the Levitical priests, who were made priests without any oath; the oath marks a higher dignity.

On "not without an oath," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note the difference: the Levitical priests received their office simply by the law's appointment, without an oath; but Christ's priesthood was confirmed by God's solemn oath — a mark of its greater permanence and dignity.

On "he was made priest," the grammatical-historical note the consequence (developed in v.22): the oath shows that Christ's priesthood is more firmly established and more enduring than the Levitical; God's oath guarantees its perpetuity and superiority. The verse displays Christ made priest with an oath, unlike the Levites.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the new point — "inasmuch as not without an oath he was made priest," Christ's priesthood established with an oath, unlike the Levites (Barnes, Robertson); the Levitical priests made without an oath, but Christ confirmed by God's oath, a mark of greater dignity (Barnes, Calvin); the oath showing his priesthood more firmly established and enduring (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the oath-confirmed priesthood. The verse marks the oath: not without it. The verse displays the contrast: with the Levites. The verse displays the confirmation: God's oath. The verse displays the dignity: greater. The point displays Christ's priesthood sworn by God. The verse displays the oath: present. The verse displays the priest: made. The verse displays the Levites: without oath. Christ made priest with an oath, unlike the Levites, displays a further mark of his superior priesthood: the Levitical priests were made without any oath, but Christ was made priest with God's solemn oath — a confirmation marking the greater dignity and permanence of his office. The verse displays the sworn priesthood, whose oath is now cited (v.21).
21:
"(For those priests were made without an oath; but this with an oath by him that said unto him, The Lord sware and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec:)" The witnesses note the oath confirming Christ's eternal priesthood. On "those priests were made without an oath; but this with an oath," the grammatical voices note the explicit contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the comparison drawn out: "those priests (the Levitical) were made without an oath; but this (Christ) with an oath" — the Levites had no oath; Christ's priesthood was sworn by God himself; the oath sets Christ's office above theirs.

On "by him that said... The Lord sware and will not repent," the witnesses note the divine swearer. Barnes and Calvin note that God himself "sware and will not repent (will not change his mind, will not revoke it)" — the oath is irrevocable; God will never retract or alter it; the priesthood it establishes is therefore unchangeable and eternal.

On "Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec," the eastern Cyril notes the sworn perpetuity: the content of the oath is the eternal Melchizedek priesthood — "a priest for ever"; God's irrevocable oath guarantees that Christ's priesthood will never pass away. The verse displays the oath confirming Christ's eternal priesthood.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "those priests (Levitical) were made without an oath; but this (Christ) with an oath," the Levites without oath, Christ's priesthood sworn by God (Barnes, Robertson); God "sware and will not repent (will not revoke it)," the oath irrevocable, the priesthood unchangeable (Barnes, Calvin); the content "a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec," the sworn eternal priesthood (Cyril). The verse displays the sworn priesthood. The verse marks the contrast: without and with an oath. The verse displays the swearer: the Lord. The verse displays the irrevocability: will not repent. The verse displays the content: a priest for ever. The oath displays Christ's priesthood eternally guaranteed. The verse displays the oath: God's. The verse displays the repenting: never. The verse displays the for ever: sworn. The oath confirming Christ's eternal priesthood displays the divine guarantee of his office: the Levitical priests were made without an oath, but Christ with God's irrevocable oath — "The Lord sware and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek" — so that his priesthood is unchangeable and everlasting. The verse displays the sworn eternal priesthood, making Jesus surety of a better covenant (v.22).
22:
"By so much was Jesus made a surety of a better testament." The brief verse notes Jesus made the surety of a better covenant. The grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note the result of the oath: "by so much (in proportion to the superiority just shown, the oath) was Jesus made a surety (egguos — a guarantor, one who pledges and secures) of a better testament (diatheke — covenant)" — because his priesthood is confirmed by oath and eternal, Jesus is the guarantor of a better covenant than the old.

On "a surety," the witnesses note the office. Barnes and Calvin note the meaning of "surety": Jesus is the one who guarantees the new covenant — he secures and makes good its promises by his own person and work; the surety pledges himself for the covenant's fulfillment, an office Jesus alone holds.

On "a better testament," the grammatical-historical note the superiority: the covenant Jesus guarantees is "better" than the old — founded on better promises, a better priesthood, and a better hope; the first mention of the "better covenant" that the following chapters develop. The verse displays Jesus made the surety of a better covenant.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conclusion — "by so much (in proportion to the oath's superiority) was Jesus made a surety (guarantor) of a better testament (covenant)," his oath-confirmed eternal priesthood making him guarantor of a better covenant (Barnes, Robertson); "surety," the one who guarantees the new covenant, securing its promises by his person and work (Barnes, Calvin); "a better testament," better than the old, founded on better promises and priesthood (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the surety. The verse marks the proportion: by so much. The verse displays the office: a surety. The verse displays the person: Jesus. The verse displays the covenant: better. The conclusion displays Jesus guaranteeing the better covenant. The verse displays the surety: Jesus. The verse displays the guarantee: of the covenant. The verse displays the testament: better. Jesus made the surety of a better covenant displays the crowning result of his sworn, eternal priesthood: because his office is confirmed by God's oath and abides for ever, Jesus is made the guarantor — the one who secures and makes good — of a covenant far better than the old. The verse displays the surety of the better covenant, whose unchangeable priesthood saves to the uttermost (vv.23-25).
23:
"And they truly were many priests, because they were not suffered to continue by reason of death:" The brief verse notes the many Levitical priests, ended by death. The grammatical voices note the succession. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast being drawn: "they truly were many priests (a long succession of high priests over the centuries), because they were not suffered to continue (prevented from continuing in office) by reason of death" — the Levitical priests were many, because death removed each in turn and a successor had to take his place.

On "many priests," the witnesses note the multitude. Barnes and Calvin note that the Levitical high-priesthood passed through many hands — a long line of mortal men, each succeeding the last; no single priest could continue, for all died.

On "by reason of death," the grammatical-historical note the cause: "by reason of death" — death was the reason for the succession; the mortality of the priests made a continual change necessary; this very mortality marks the weakness of the Levitical priesthood over against Christ's. The verse displays the many Levitical priests, ended by death.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "they truly were many priests (a long succession), because they were not suffered to continue (prevented) by reason of death," the priests many because death removed each in turn (Barnes, Robertson); the high-priesthood passing through many mortal hands, none continuing (Barnes, Calvin); "by reason of death," mortality the cause of the succession, marking the priesthood's weakness (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the many priests. The verse marks the number: many priests. The verse displays the cause: death. The verse displays the succession: each replaced. The verse displays the weakness: mortality. The contrast displays the mortal priests in long succession. The verse displays the priests: many. The verse displays the death: removing them. The verse displays the continuing: prevented. The many Levitical priests, ended by death, display the weakness of the old priesthood: they were many, a long succession, because death prevented any from continuing — each mortal priest removed in turn and replaced; their very mortality marking the contrast with the abiding Christ. The verse displays the dying priests, over against the unchangeable priesthood of Christ (v.24).
24:
"But this man, because he continueth ever, hath an unchangeable priesthood." The brief verse notes Christ's unchangeable priesthood, because he continues forever. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the great difference: "but this man (Christ), because he continueth ever (abides for ever, never dying), hath an unchangeable (aparabatos — that does not pass to another, untransferable, permanent) priesthood" — unlike the dying Levites, Christ lives for ever, and so his priesthood never passes to a successor.

On "because he continueth ever," the witnesses note the perpetuity. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ's endless life (v.16) is the ground: because he never dies, he never needs a successor; his priesthood is held by him alone, perpetually.

On "an unchangeable priesthood," the eastern Cyril notes the meaning: "unchangeable (aparabatos)" means untransferable — a priesthood that does not pass from him to another, as the Levitical passed from each dying priest to the next; Christ holds it inviolably and for ever. The verse displays Christ's unchangeable priesthood.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "this man (Christ), because he continueth ever (abides for ever), hath an unchangeable (untransferable, permanent) priesthood," Christ living for ever, his priesthood never passing to a successor (Barnes, Robertson); his endless life the ground, never needing a successor (Barnes, Calvin); "unchangeable" meaning untransferable, held by him alone for ever (Cyril). The verse displays the unchangeable priesthood. The verse marks the contrast: but this man. The verse displays the ground: he continueth ever. The verse displays the priesthood: unchangeable. The verse displays the meaning: untransferable. The contrast displays Christ's perpetual, untransferable priesthood. The verse displays the continuing: for ever. The verse displays the priesthood: permanent. The verse displays the change: none. Christ's unchangeable priesthood, because he continues forever, displays the crowning superiority of his office: unlike the many dying Levites whose priesthood passed from one to another, Christ abides for ever and so holds a priesthood that never passes to a successor — untransferable, permanent, and eternal. The verse displays the abiding priesthood, by which he saves to the uttermost (v.25).
25:
"Wherefore he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them." The witnesses note Christ able to save to the uttermost, ever living to intercede. On "he is able also to save them to the uttermost," the grammatical voices note the saving power. Barnes and Robertson note the glorious conclusion: "he is able (has the power) also to save them to the uttermost (eis to panteles — completely, perfectly, to the very end, and for all time) that come unto God by him" — because his priesthood is eternal, Christ can save completely and for ever all who approach God through him; nothing is lacking in his salvation.

On "to the uttermost," the witnesses note the fullness. Barnes and Calvin note the rich phrase: "to the uttermost" — wholly, perfectly, to the end; whether in degree (completely) or in time (for ever), his salvation is total and final; he saves not partially but entirely, and keeps to the end.

On "seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the ground: "he ever liveth (lives perpetually) to make intercession (entynchanein — to plead, intercede, appear on their behalf) for them" — the ground of his complete saving power is his unending life and ceaseless intercession; the living priest ever pleads for his people at God's right hand, so their salvation is secure. The verse displays Christ able to save to the uttermost, ever living to intercede.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the saving power — "he is able to save them to the uttermost (completely, for ever) that come unto God by him," Christ saving completely all who approach through him (Barnes, Robertson); "to the uttermost" wholly and to the end, total and final salvation (Barnes, Calvin); "seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them," his unending life and ceaseless intercession the ground, the living priest pleading for his people (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the uttermost salvation. The verse marks the power: able to save. The verse displays the fullness: to the uttermost. The verse displays the comers: through him. The verse displays the ground: ever living to intercede. The conclusion displays Christ fully saving through his endless intercession. The verse displays the saving: complete. The verse displays the living: for ever. The verse displays the intercession: ceaseless. Christ able to save to the uttermost, ever living to intercede, displays the crowning glory of his eternal priesthood: because he lives for ever and ceaselessly intercedes, he can save completely and to the very end all who come to God through him — his unending life and intercession the unfailing ground of his people's full salvation. The verse displays the uttermost-saving priest, holy and exalted above the heavens (v.26).
26:
"For such an high priest became us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and made higher than the heavens;" The witnesses note the sinless, exalted high priest fitted to our need. On "such an high priest became us," the grammatical voices note the fitness. Barnes and Robertson note the suitability: "such an high priest became (was fitting for, suited) us" — a high priest of this character is exactly what our need required; his perfections answer to our necessities.

On "holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners," the witnesses note the sinless character. Barnes and Calvin note the fourfold description: "holy (hosios — pious, devoted to God), harmless (akakos — innocent, free from malice or guile), undefiled (amiantos — pure, unstained by sin), separate from sinners (set apart, distinct from sinful men in his moral purity)" — Christ's perfect sinlessness, unlike the Levitical priests who had to offer for their own sins (v.27).

On "made higher than the heavens," the puritan Henry notes the exaltation: "made higher than the heavens (exalted above all heavens, to God's right hand)" — Christ is not only sinless but exalted above the created heavens; his exaltation matches his purity. The verse displays the sinless, exalted high priest fitted to our need.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fitness — "such an high priest became (was fitting for) us," his character exactly answering our need (Barnes, Robertson); the fourfold sinlessness "holy (devoted to God), harmless (innocent), undefiled (unstained), separate from sinners (morally distinct)," Christ's perfect purity unlike the Levites (Barnes, Calvin); "made higher than the heavens," exalted above all heavens to God's right hand (Henry). The verse displays the perfect high priest. The verse marks the fitness: became us. The verse displays the purity: holy, harmless, undefiled. The verse displays the separation: from sinners. The verse displays the exaltation: higher than the heavens. The description displays the sinless, exalted priest suited to us. The verse displays the holiness: complete. The verse displays the sinlessness: perfect. The verse displays the exaltation: supreme. The sinless, exalted high priest fitted to our need displays the perfections of Christ our priest: holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and exalted higher than the heavens — exactly the high priest our need required, whose spotless purity and supreme exaltation answer our necessities. The verse displays the perfect priest, who needs no daily sacrifice for himself (v.27).
27:
"Who needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins, and then for the people's: for this he did once, when he offered up himself." The witnesses note Christ offering himself once, unlike the daily Levitical sacrifices. On "who needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the difference from the Levitical priests: "who needeth not daily (or, as the high priest yearly on the Day of Atonement, the 'daily' covering the regular recurring sacrifices), as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins" — the Levitical high priest, being himself a sinner, had to offer first for his own sins before he could offer for the people.

On "and then for the people's," the witnesses note the repeated offering. Barnes and Calvin note that the Levitical priest offered "for the people's (sins)" repeatedly — the sacrifices had to be offered again and again, never finally taking away sin; the repetition proved their insufficiency.

On "for this he did once, when he offered up himself," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the once-for-all sacrifice: Christ "did this once (hapax — once for all), when he offered up himself" — needing no sacrifice for his own sins (being sinless), Christ offered, not an animal, but himself, and that once for all; his single, perfect self-offering accomplished what the repeated Levitical sacrifices could not. The verse displays Christ offering himself once, unlike the daily Levitical sacrifices.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "who needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins," the Levitical priest, a sinner, offering first for himself (Barnes, Robertson); "and then for the people's," the sacrifices repeated again and again, never finally taking away sin (Barnes, Calvin); "this he did once, when he offered up himself," Christ, sinless, offering himself once for all, his single offering accomplishing what the repeated ones could not (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the once-for-all offering. The verse marks the Levitical: daily sacrifice for self and people. The verse displays the repetition: again and again. The verse displays Christ: offered once. The verse displays the offering: himself. The contrast displays Christ's single self-offering surpassing the repeated sacrifices. The verse displays the Levites: repeating. The verse displays the Christ: once. The verse displays the self-offering: perfect. Christ offering himself once, unlike the daily Levitical sacrifices, displays the surpassing efficacy of his priesthood: the Levitical priests, being sinners, offered repeatedly first for themselves and then the people, never finishing the work; but Christ, sinless, offered himself once for all — a single, perfect sacrifice that needs no repetition. The verse displays the once-for-all self-offering, consecrated for evermore (v.28).
28:
"For the law maketh men high priests which have infirmity; but the word of the oath, which was since the law, maketh the Son, who is consecrated for evermore." The witnesses note the contrast: the law's weak priests, the oath's perfected Son. On "the law maketh men high priests which have infirmity," the grammatical voices note the weak priests. Barnes and Robertson note the summary contrast: "the law maketh (appoints) men high priests which have infirmity (astheneian — weakness, moral and physical frailty, including sinfulness and mortality)" — the law appointed weak, sinful, mortal men as high priests.

On "the word of the oath, which was since the law," the witnesses note the later oath. Barnes and Calvin note that "the word of the oath (Psalm 110:4, God's sworn declaration), which was since (after, later than) the law" — the oath, coming after the law, supersedes it; the later word of the oath appoints a different and better priest.

On "maketh the Son, who is consecrated for evermore," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the perfected Son: the oath "maketh the Son (not weak men, but the Son of God), who is consecrated (teteleiomenon — perfected, made complete, fully qualified as priest) for evermore (for ever)" — the oath appoints the eternal Son, perfected through his sufferings (2:10; 5:9) and consecrated as priest for ever; the contrast is complete — weak men by the law, the perfected Son by the oath. The verse displays the contrast: the law's weak priests, the oath's perfected Son.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "the law maketh (appoints) men high priests which have infirmity (weakness, frailty, sinfulness, mortality)," the law appointing weak, mortal men (Barnes, Robertson); "the word of the oath, which was since (after) the law," the oath coming later and superseding the law (Barnes, Calvin); "maketh the Son, who is consecrated (perfected, fully qualified) for evermore," the oath appointing the eternal Son, perfected and consecrated priest for ever (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the final contrast. The verse marks the law: weak men. The verse displays the infirmity: their frailty. The verse displays the oath: later, better. The verse displays the Son: perfected for ever. The contrast displays the weak priests of law and the perfected Son of the oath. The verse displays the law: appointing men. The verse displays the oath: appointing the Son. The verse displays the consecration: for evermore. The contrast — the law's weak priests, the oath's perfected Son — displays the summary of the whole argument: the law appointed weak, sinful, mortal men as high priests, but the word of the oath, coming after the law, appoints the Son of God himself, perfected through his sufferings and consecrated a priest for evermore. The verse displays the perfected Son, the eternal high priest, closing the great chapter on the priesthood after the order of Melchizedek.
